Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Originating Claim No 246 of 2023
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Concorde Services Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Concorde Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 26 May 2010 with its principal activity listed as beauty salons and spas, and manpower contracting services.
On incorporation of Concorde Services Pte Ltd, Mr Chua Swee Kheng and Mr Ong Kim Hock were the only two directors and shareholders, each holding half of the shares.
A bank account in the name of Concorde Services Pte Ltd was opened with United Overseas Bank Ltd, referred to as the Concorde Bank Account.
Upon the incorporation of Concorde Services Pte Ltd, Mr Chua deposited $25,000 into the Concorde Bank Account to facilitate miscellaneous business expenses.
Sometime in August 2011, a tender was submitted on behalf of Concorde Services Pte Ltd to lease a space at Sembawang MRT station, referred to as the Premises.

The Singapore Mass Rapid Transit Corporation awarded the tender to Concorde Services Pte Ltd and a lease agreement, known as the Claimant’s Lease, was entered into between SMRT and Concorde Services Pte Ltd for 36 months commencing on 16 October 2011 at a monthly rental of $15,000.
On 2 September 2011, Concorde Services Pte Ltd registered a sole proprietorship named Station 33 to carry out a hairstyling business, with Station 33 wholly owned by Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
In November 2011, Station 33 commenced its hairstyling business at the Premises in Sembawang MRT station.
From November 2011, Station 33’s earnings were deposited into a United Overseas Bank account referred to as the Station 33 Bank Account.
The Station 33 Bank Account with United Overseas Bank Ltd listed Mr Ong Kim Hock and Mdm Lim Ping Ping as signatories.
Mr Chua and Mr Ong each deposited $100,000 into the Station 33 Bank Account as initial equity for Station 33.

On 23 October 2011, an agreement was prepared setting out the remuneration structure for Mr Ong Kim Hock in relation to Station 33, stating that if the company was profitable the first $5,000 net per month would be paid to him, and if the company made a loss he would receive $2,000 net per month.
For November 2011, bank records Station 33’s cash takings of approximately $18,979.40 were deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account.
For December 2011, bank records Station 33’s cash takings of approximately $23,798.37 were deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account.

From Station 33’s registration on 2 September 2011 until December 2011, Mdm Lim Ping Ping managed the accounting and finances for Station 33, including financial records for November and December 2011.

Mr Chua formed a backend operations team under Concorde Services Pte Ltd to handle Station 33’s administration, comprising Mdm Lim Ping Ping, Ms Shirley Chee Fung Mei, Ms Belinda Ng, and Mr Selvakumar A/L Marimuth.
After December 2011, Mr Peter Chong was engaged to take over the accounting work for Station 33, preparing subsequent financial documentation for Station 33.
Before 2010, Mr Chua and Mr Ong became acquainted during Singapore national service and/or reservist obligations and later agreed around 2010 to start a hairstyling and salon business together.

From the start of 2012, Station 33’s cash takings were collected in cash at the Premises by staff including Mr Ong, Mr Peter Chong, or other hairstylists and were not deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account.
A point-of-sale system capable of logging cash takings was set up at Station 33 but was not used for recording cash transactions.

On 13 February 2012, a tenancy agreement referred to as the Subletting Agreement was entered into between Mr Ong Kim Hock and Ye Lizhen for Ye Lizhen to operate a business known as J.C. Skin in part of the Premises.

Under the Subletting Agreement, rental payments from J.C. Skin were made by cheque to Mr Ong, with four months of rental totaling $20,000 transferred to the Station 33 Bank Account and remaining rental payments deposited into Mr Ong’s personal bank account.
The latest available cheque for rental payment from J.C. Skin to Mr Ong under the Subletting Agreement is dated 10 May 2013.

On 1 April 2013, Mr Selvakumar sent an email to Station 33’s email address addressed to Mr Peter Chong requesting that all Station 33 documents be brought back to the salon and filed, and stating that a police report would be considered if this was not done.

On the morning of 20 April 2013, Mr Peter Chong emailed Mr Selvakumar attaching Station 33’s general ledger details from 1 January 2011 to 20 April 2013 and requested assistance in generating other reports.
On 20 April 2013, Mr Peter Chong forwarded to Mr Selvakumar balance sheets, general ledgers from 2011 to 31 March 2013, and three profit and loss statements for Station 33.

On 7 March 2015, Mr Chua sent a letter to Station 33 addressed to Mr Ong requesting financial and management records for Station 33 for 2012, 2013, and 2014, information on the 2014 renewal of the Claimant’s Lease, details of the Subletting Agreement, and employees’ records including CPF and levy payment records, with a 21-day deadline.

On 20 April 2015, Mr Chua sent a follow-up letter to Mr Ong granting 30 days from the date of the letter to submit the requested documents and indicating that further action would be considered for non-compliance.

On 15 July 2015, Mr Chua sent a letter to Mr Ong enclosing a letter from the Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore requesting submission of income tax Form C-S for year of assessment 2014 by 10 August 2015 and a summons letter from the State Courts requiring attendance.

On 25 July 2016, the Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore issued a letter to Concorde Services Pte Ltd offering to compound an offence for failing to submit employees’ income information for Station 33 for year of assessment 2016 and enclosing a summons for court attendance.

On 13 October 2017, Mr Chua sent a letter on behalf of Concorde Services Pte Ltd to Mr Ong stating that records of Station 33 had not been submitted despite reminders, that statutory non-compliance had led to actions by authorities, and authorising the company’s accountant or auditor, Ms Shirley, to take over all documents and files related to Station 33 on 14 October 2017.
Security cameras were installed near the sales counter and cash register area at Station 33 when the business commenced operations in November 2011.
By early 2012, the original CCTV monitoring system near the sales counter at Station 33 was dismantled and replaced, and remote access previously available to Mr Chua was no longer available.
In June 2012, a payment voucher recorded that Mr Ong received a total of $8,091.20 from Station 33 comprising $3,000 basic pay, $700 overtime, and $4,391.20 commission, before CPF deductions.
In December 2012, a payment voucher recorded that Mr Ong received a total of $6,160.75 from Station 33 comprising $3,000 basic pay and $3,160.75 commission, before CPF deductions.
Station 33’s business registration lapsed on 2 September 2016 and was not renewed by either Mr Chua or Mr Ong.
On 29 July 2017, Station 33’s business registration was cancelled following the lapse of its registration.
In May 2014, the Claimant’s Lease for the Premises was renewed for another 36 months from 16 October 2014 at an increased monthly rental of $17,000.
The Claimant’s Lease between SMRT and Concorde Services Pte Ltd expired and was terminated on 16 October 2017.
In 2011, A@risco Services Pte Ltd carried out renovation works at the Premises for Concorde Services Pte Ltd for the sum of $60,852.97, excluding interest and later application costs.
On 17 January 2020, Concorde Services Pte Ltd was placed into compulsory winding up and went into liquidation following a winding up application by A@risco Services Pte Ltd.
On 17 January 2020, the High Court issued order HC/ORC 487/2020 in HC/CWU 404/2019 for the compulsory winding up of Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
From early 2012, Mr Chua did not physically return to the Premises of Station 33.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and financial flows, the business operations, cash takings, subletting income, and lease arrangements relating to Station 33 and the Premises resulted in diversion or non-accounting of Station 33’s assets away from Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors and persons controlling a company’s business are generally manage company assets and business opportunities for the benefit of the company, to keep proper financial records, and to ensure that company income, including cash takings and subletting income from company premises, is properly recorded and channelled through company accounts rather than personal accounts.
Party_Details.Application: Concorde Services Pte Ltd owned Station 33 and held the lease of the Premises. Station 33’s earnings were initially deposited into a bank account where both investors contributed equal capital. From January 2012, cash takings were collected at the Premises but no longer deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account, while a point-of-sale system capable of logging cash was not used. A subletting arrangement for part of the Premises was documented in a tenancy agreement signed personally by Mr Ong, with only part of the rental transferred to the Station 33 Bank Account and the remainder paid into his personal account. Security cameras monitoring the cash register area were dismantled and replaced, removing remote visibility previously available to Mr Chua. Payment vouchers Mr Ong’s recorded remuneration from Station 33 in mid and late 2012 exceeded the fixed amounts set out in the October 2011 remuneration agreement. Backend staff repeatedly requested accounting records and financial statements, and multiple letters from Mr Chua sought financial and management records, lease documents, and employee records over several years. Station 33’s registration later lapsed and was cancelled, and Concorde Services Pte Ltd was eventually wound up after an unpaid renovation debt. These pre-dispute events describe how income streams and records relating to Station 33 and the Premises were handled and how Concorde’s business structure and finances were organised before litigation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the first defendant owed fiduciary duties as a director of the claimant, rejected his claim of ignorance of his directorship, and held that he was aware of his role from the outset. The judge considered the disappearance of cash deposits after December 2011, the dismantling of CCTV cameras, the use of a personal tenancy agreement for subletting, and the routing of most subletting rental into the first defendant’s personal account as part of a coordinated pattern of conduct. The court found that the first defendant treated Station 33’s cash takings as his own, failed to account for them, and misapplied Station 33’s assets, including by transferring business, assets, and goodwill to the second defendant. The judge rejected the narrative that Mr Chua abandoned the business and found Mr Chua’s evidence more cogent. The court concluded that the first defendant breached fiduciary duties owed to the claimant, that the second defendant was a knowing recipient of misapplied assets, and that there was an unlawful means conspiracy. The limitation defence was held not to apply because it was not pleaded and, in any event, the court considered that the relevant limitation provisions would not bar the claims given the nature and timing of the breaches and the classification of the first defendant as a Class 1 constructive trustee. The court then proceeded to assess damages, including categories relating to cash takings, rental income from subletting, conversion of assets, and various expense offsets, and entered judgment on liability and damages in favour of the claimant.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Originating Claim No 246 of 2023
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ong Kim Hock

Facts.Text: From 10 April 2002 to sometime in 2010, Mr Ong Kim Hock operated his own hairstyling studio under the name Andy Ong Hair Studio.
Mr Ong and Mr Chua became acquainted during Singapore national service and/or reservist obligations and later agreed around 2010 to start a hairstyling and salon business together.
Sometime in 2010, Mr Ong closed down Andy Ong Hair Studio and agreed with Mr Chua to start a new hairstyling and salon business.
On 26 May 2010, Mr Ong became a director and 50% shareholder of Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
By mid-June 2010, Mr Ong was a signatory to the Concorde Bank Account opened with United Overseas Bank Ltd for Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
From November 2011, Mr Ong was mainly responsible for Station 33’s day-to-day operations and worked as the head hairstylist at Station 33.
In November 2011, Mr Ong and Mr Chua each deposited $100,000 into the Station 33 Bank Account as initial equity for Station 33.

From the start of 2012, cash payments from Station 33 customers were collected at the Premises by Mr Ong, Mr Peter Chong, or other hairstylists and were not deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account.
On 13 February 2012, Mr Ong signed a tenancy agreement with Ye Lizhen for J.C. Skin to sublet part of the Premises, with the agreement naming him personally as tenant.

Under the Subletting Agreement, rental cheques from J.C. Skin were issued to Mr Ong, with four months of rental totaling $20,000 transferred to the Station 33 Bank Account and the remaining rental amounts deposited into Mr Ong’s personal bank account.
The latest available rental cheque from J.C. Skin to Mr Ong under the Subletting Agreement is dated 10 May 2013.

By early 2012, the original CCTV cameras at Station 33 near the sales counter and cash register were dismantled and replaced, and remote access previously available to Mr Chua was no longer available, while Mr Ong continued to manage the Premises.
After December 2011, Mr Ong introduced his acquaintance, Mr Peter Chong, to take over accounting work for Station 33, replacing Mdm Lim in that function.
In June 2012, a Station 33 payment voucher recorded that Mr Ong received $8,091.20 comprising $3,000 basic pay, $700 overtime, and $4,391.20 commission, before CPF deductions.
In December 2012, a Station 33 payment voucher recorded that Mr Ong received $6,160.75 comprising $3,000 basic pay and $3,160.75 commission, before CPF deductions.

On 20 April 2013, Mr Ong’s associate, Mr Peter Chong, emailed Station 33 financial documents including general ledgers and profit and loss statements to Mr Selvakumar while Mr Ong continued to manage Station 33’s operations.
In May 2014, Mr Ong arranged for the renewal of the Claimant’s Lease of the Premises for another 36 months from 16 October 2014 at a monthly rental of $17,000.

On 7 March 2015, Mr Chua sent a letter to Station 33 addressed to Mr Ong requesting Station 33’s financial and management records for 2012 to 2014, details of the 2014 lease renewal, details of the subletting arrangement, and employee records.
On 20 April 2015, Mr Chua sent a follow-up letter to Mr Ong granting 30 days to submit the requested Station 33 documents and indicating that further action would be considered for non-compliance.

On 15 July 2015, Mr Chua sent a letter to Mr Ong enclosing correspondence from the Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore and a State Courts summons relating to Concorde Services Pte Ltd’s tax and filing matters.

On 13 October 2017, Mr Chua sent a letter to Mr Ong stating that Station 33 records had not been submitted despite reminders and authorising the company’s accountant or auditor to take over all Station 33 documents and files on 14 October 2017.

On 3 April 2017, Mr Ong submitted a tender for the lease of the Premises under the name Station 33, indicating in the remarks that there would be a change of company name upon successful tender due to different ownership of the business.
On 6 April 2017, SMRT sent a letter by courier to the Premises stating that the Claimant’s Lease would be terminated upon its expiry on 16 October 2017.
On 25 April 2017, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was incorporated with Mr Ong’s involvement to operate a hair salon business.

After incorporation on 25 April 2017, the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority profile of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was provided to SMRT to facilitate a lease agreement for the Premises.
Between 12 April and 22 May 2017, SMRT exchanged emails with Mr Ong and/or his nephew, Mr Johnson Ong, discussing key commercial terms for the tender of the Premises.

On 27 June 2017, SMRT sent a letter to Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd offering a lease of the Premises for 36 months from 19 October 2017 with monthly rental of $18,300 for the first year, $18,500 for the second year, and $18,700 for the third year, plus 0.5% of monthly gross sales.

On 19 October 2017, a lease between SMRT and Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd for the Premises commenced for 36 months, following a three-day fitting period for minor renovation and signboard changes.
From 19 October 2017, Mr Ong operated a hair salon business at the Premises through Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd, following the expiry of the Claimant’s Lease on 16 October 2017.

On 1 June 2019, Mr Chua lodged a police report concerning Mr Ong’s act of tendering for the lease of the Premises under the name of Concorde’s business and subsequently changing the lease to Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd.

On 4 October 2020, the lease of the Premises held by Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was renewed for another 36 months from 19 October 2020 to 18 October 2023 at discounted monthly rentals of $9,800 for the first year, $10,600 for the second year, and $11,700 for the third year.
On 11 May 2023, the lease of the Premises held by Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was renewed again for 36 months from 19 October 2023 to 18 October 2026 at a monthly rental of $13,688.
From October 2017 to May 2022, some outgoing expenses of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd were paid from the Station 33 Bank Account, with the total amount of such payments recorded as $15,801.24.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, Mr Ong’s management of Station 33’s cash takings, subletting income, remuneration, and subsequent use of the Premises through Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd resulted in Station 33’s and Concorde’s business income and assets being diverted or not recorded through company channels.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who control a company’s operations and bank accounts are generally channel business income, including cash takings and rental from use of company premises, into company accounts, to keep accurate financial records, and to avoid using company-related income streams and opportunities for separate personal or external corporate benefit without clear company authorisation.
Party_Details.Application: Mr Ong was a director and equal shareholder of Concorde Services Pte Ltd and managed Station 33’s day-to-day operations. He was a signatory to both the Concorde Bank Account and the Station 33 Bank Account, into which he and Mr Chua each deposited $100,000 as initial equity. In November and December 2011, Station 33’s cash takings were deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account, but from January 2012 onward, cash takings were collected at the Premises and not deposited into that account, while a point-of-sale system capable of logging cash was not used. In February 2012, Mr Ong personally signed a tenancy agreement to sublet part of the Premises to J.C. Skin, with only part of the rental transferred to the Station 33 Bank Account and the remainder deposited into his personal bank account. Around the same period, CCTV cameras monitoring the cash register area were dismantled and replaced, and accounting responsibilities were shifted from Mdm Lim to Mr Ong’s acquaintance, Mr Peter Chong. Payment vouchers Mr Ong’s recorded remuneration in mid and late 2012 exceeded the fixed amounts set out in the October 2011 remuneration agreement. Over subsequent years, Mr Chua and backend staff sent multiple letters and emails requesting Station 33’s financial records, lease documents, and employee records. In April 2017, Mr Ong submitted a tender for the Premises under Station 33’s name with an indication that the company name would change upon successful tender, and shortly thereafter incorporated Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd, which then entered into a lease with SMRT commencing three days after the expiry of the Claimant’s Lease. From October 2017 to May 2022, some expenses of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd were paid from the Station 33 Bank Account. These events describe how Mr Ong handled Station 33’s income streams, lease opportunities, and financial flows before the dispute and how he transitioned the use of the Premises to a new company under his control.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court rejected Mr Ong’s claim that he was unaware of his directorship in Concorde Services Pte Ltd, noting his own acknowledgments of the company’s role, his status as a bank signatory, and his statements about asking to be removed as a director. The judge found that the corporate structure did not disadvantage him and that his narrative of being misled was illogical. The court held that Mr Ong had sufficient English proficiency to understand corporate documents and that his portrayal of himself as barely literate was not credible. On the facts, the judge concluded that from early 2012 Mr Ong took control of Station 33’s cash takings, stopped depositing cash into the Station 33 Bank Account, dismantled CCTV cameras monitoring the cash register, and arranged a subletting agreement in his personal name with most rental paid into his personal account. The court found that his remuneration exceeded the agreed structure and that he effectively treated Station 33’s cash as his own. The judge preferred Mr Chua’s evidence over Mr Ong’s, rejected the suggestion that Mr Chua abandoned the business, and found that Mr Ong misapplied Station 33’s assets and diverted business and lease opportunities to Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd. The court held that these actions constituted breaches of fiduciary duties and supported liability for knowing receipt and unlawful means conspiracy, and it accepted that $15,801.24 of expenses paid from the Station 33 Bank Account for the second defendant’s benefit should be restored. The limitation defence was rejected as unpleaded and, in any event, inapplicable given the classification of Mr Ong as a Class 1 constructive trustee and the timing of the events.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Originating Claim No 246 of 2023
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 25 April 2017 with involvement from Mr Ong Kim Hock to operate a hair salon business.

Following its incorporation on 25 April 2017, the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority profile of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was provided to SMRT to facilitate a lease agreement for the Premises.

On 27 June 2017, SMRT issued a letter to Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd offering a lease of the Premises for 36 months from 19 October 2017 with monthly rental of $18,300 for the first year, $18,500 for the second year, and $18,700 for the third year, plus 0.5% of monthly gross sales.

On 19 October 2017, a lease between SMRT and Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd for the Premises commenced for 36 months, following a three-day fitting period for minor renovation and signboard changes.
From 19 October 2017, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd operated a hair salon business at the Premises previously used by Station 33.

Between October 2017 and May 2022, some outgoing expenses of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd were paid from the Station 33 Bank Account, with the total amount of such payments recorded as $15,801.24.

On 4 October 2020, the lease of the Premises held by Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was renewed for another 36 months from 19 October 2020 to 18 October 2023 at discounted monthly rentals of $9,800 for the first year, $10,600 for the second year, and $11,700 for the third year.
On 11 May 2023, the lease of the Premises held by Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was renewed again for 36 months from 19 October 2023 to 18 October 2026 at a monthly rental of $13,688.
At the time of the trial in July 2024, the Premises remained leased under the name of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd’s acquisition and use of the lease of the Premises and its receipt of payments funded from the Station 33 Bank Account involved receipt of value originating from Station 33’s and Concorde’s business.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that receives assets, payments, or business opportunities traceable to another entity’s business and bank accounts may be treated as having received value originating from that other entity, particularly where the same individual controls both entities and the assets or payments are drawn from the earlier entity’s accounts.
Party_Details.Application: Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 25 April 2017 while Station 33 was still operating at the Premises under a lease held by Concorde Services Pte Ltd. Shortly after a tender submission under Station 33’s name, SMRT offered a lease directly to Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd, which commenced on 19 October 2017, three days after the expiry of the Claimant’s Lease. The new company continued operating a hair salon at the same Premises. From October 2017 to May 2022, some outgoing expenses of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd were paid from the Station 33 Bank Account, with a recorded total of $15,801.24. The company’s lease was renewed twice, in 2020 and 2023, and it continued to occupy the Premises at the time of trial. These pre-dispute events Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd obtained the lease and operations at the Premises previously used by Station 33 and that some of its expenses were funded from an account associated with Station 33.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd as the vehicle through which Mr Ong continued the hair salon business at the Premises after the expiry of the Claimant’s Lease. The judge found that the tender for the Premises was submitted under Station 33’s name with an indication of a forthcoming change of company name, and that shortly thereafter the second defendant was incorporated and granted a lease commencing three days after the Claimant’s Lease ended. The court noted that some of the second defendant’s expenses were paid from the Station 33 Bank Account and that Mr Ong accepted that $15,801.24 should be restored. On the evidence, the judge concluded that the second defendant received business, assets, and benefits traceable to Station 33 and the claimant, and held it liable as a knowing recipient and as part of an unlawful means conspiracy with Mr Ong. The reasoning linked the continuity of operations at the Premises, the timing of incorporation and lease grants, and the use of Station 33’s bank account to support the finding of liability.
